title: Constitutive relations for colloidal gel
authors: Saikat Roy, Yezaz Ahmed Gadi Man
abstract: The theoretical treatment of depletion gels with central interactions often involves expanding the free energy around a stress-free reference state to derive a constitutive relation between global stress and strain. The premise upon which the previous continuum theories are based, i.e., the stress-free reference state and the affine deformation, both of which do not hold in the context of amorphous gel materials. Gels never reach a true global minimum in the potential energy landscape and contain local regions of significant compressive and tensile stress, interspersed with zero-stress regions. Hence, expansion of free energy around a stressed reference state will produce scalar terms in harmonic expansion, the effects of which are qualitatively different from the terms appearing in the expansion around an unstressed reference state. In this study, we demonstrate the limitations of traditional continuum theories and propose simple constitutive relations that better capture the mechanical response of gel materials. The robustness of the proposed relations is established through large-scale numerical simulations of depletion and frictional gels across a vast parameter space.
summary: This paper addresses the failure of traditional continuum mechanics to describe the mechanical response of colloidal gels. By accounting for the quenched internal stresses present in amorphous materials, the authors develop new constitutive relations that accurately predict macroscopic stress components and Normal Stress Differences. Through large-scale simulations, they show that stress anisotropy is driven by the force network rather than the contact network. This work provides a more robust framework for understanding the rheology of complex, disordered soft matter.
main_problem: Existing continuum theories for colloidal gels fail because they assume a stress-free reference state and affine deformation, which do not account for the inherent quenched internal stresses and structural inhomogeneities in amorphous gels.
main_result: The authors propose new constitutive relations that relate macroscopic stress to internal state parameters (force and contact anisotropy), successfully capturing the evolution of Normal Stress Difference (NSD) and demonstrating that stress anisotropy originates from the force network rather than the contact network.
method: Large-scale 3D Langevin dynamics simulations of depletion and frictional gels using the LAMMPS code, combined with a mathematical framework involving spherical harmonic expansions of contact and force distributions.
model_or_system: The study uses two models of colloidal gels: depletion gels (modeled with a Morse potential for short-range attraction) and frictional gels (modeled via the Discrete Element Method with non-hydrodynamic contact friction).
key_observables: Shear modulus (Gc), axial and transverse stresses (sigma_zz, sigma_xx, sigma_yy), Normal Stress Difference (N1, N2), contact orientation distribution, and anisotropy parameters (a, an, at).
important_parameters: Volume fraction (phi), attraction strength (beta U0), coordination number (zc), and the distinction between central (depletion) and non-central (frictional) interactions.
main_assumptions: The contact network is assumed to be nearly isotropic; hydrodynamic forces are neglected in the quasistatic limit; and pairwise interaction forces are assumed to be statistically independent of branch vectors.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows contact orientation and force angular modulation; Figure 2 compares anisotropy parameter evolution; Figures 4-7 show gel structure snapshots, static structure factors, and probability density functions for bond orientations.
paper_structure: The paper begins by critiquing traditional continuum theories, introduces the proposed constitutive relations based on stressed reference states, describes the simulation models for depletion and frictional gels, presents results on stress anisotropy and force network modulation, and concludes with the validation of the new relations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Contact orientation distribution is plotted on a unit sphere for the depletion gel at ϕ = 0.25 for βU0 = 50. (b) The same distribution is plotted for the frictional gel at identical conditions. Isotropy in contact orientations is also maintained at higher values of attraction strengths and vol- ume fractions (see [19]). (c)-(d) show the universality in an- gular modulation of the normal forces irrespective of the fric- tional interactions or lack thereof. (e) depicts the azimuthally symmetric sinusoidal modulation of ftθ(θ, ϕ) (f) Negligible isotropic background value as observed for ftϕ(θ, ϕ).

FIG. 2. (a) Evolution of contact anisotropy, a, and normal force anisotropy parameter, an, is shown as a function of volume fraction for gels with central and non-central inter- actions. In addition, the evolution of the tangential force anisotropy, at, is presented for the frictional gel. Here, βU0 = 50. Similar values are observed for higher attraction strengths. (b) Comparison of dimensionless NSD values as obtained in the simulation with those of the model predic- tion. Here, βU0 = 50. Model comparison is also remarkable for a higher βU0 (not shown).

FIG. 3. Comparison between the prediction of the proposed model and the simulation observations for the relevant stress components covering a wide range of volume fractions, attrac- tion strengths, and the nature of interparticle interactions.

FIG. 5. (a) S(q) is plotted for the depletion gel system for two different values of attraction strengths βU0 = 50 and 100 at the dilute and the dense regime. (b) S(q) is plotted for the frictional gel at the identical conditions.

FIG. 4. (a) Illustration of typical depletion gel structure at ϕ = 0.25 for βU0 = 50. (b) The gel network for the fric- tional case at the identical conditions. (c)-(d) shows the gel structure in a thin slice for the depletion and frictional gels, respectively.

FIG. 6. Bond orientation probability density function, P(θ, ϕ) for the depletion gel is plotted at different stages of the consol- idation process and for different values of the potential mini- mum, βU0 = 50 and 100.
FIG. 7. Contact orientation probability density function, P(θ, ϕ) for the frictional gel is plotted for different volume fractions and varied attraction strengths, βU0 = 50 and 100.
